questions.question: Why is separating control plane from data plane a useful system-design habit?
options: It clarifies which logic must stay fast per request and which logic can be centralized and eventually propagated.
It only matters for operating systems.
It removes the need for policy versioning.
It makes rollout risk disappear.
questions.explanation: The distinction helps preserve latency and resilience while still supporting centralized policy.

questions.question: What is the main reason data planes often cache compiled policy locally?
options: To avoid reaching back to the control plane on the hot path for every request.
To guarantee that policies are never stale.
To avoid any need for validation.
To ensure only one node can make decisions.
questions.explanation: Local cached policy keeps request decisions fast and resilient.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with local policy bundles in the data plane?
options: They keep latency low, but introduce bounded staleness and version-skew management.
They require no rollout process.
They eliminate the control plane completely.
They make explanations impossible.
questions.explanation: Fast local decisions are valuable, but policy freshness is no longer perfect.

questions.question: Which metric most directly reveals inconsistent fleet behavior after a rollout?
options: Bundle or policy version skew across nodes and POPs.
The number of open tabs in a browser.
Average file count in the repository.
Only total global traffic.
questions.explanation: Version divergence is the clearest signal that some nodes are enforcing different rules.

questions.question: Why is staged rollout important for control-plane changes?
options: A bad policy bundle can otherwise become a global outage or mass reject event very quickly.
It guarantees no human ever makes a mistake.
It removes all need for rollback.
It only matters for low-QPS systems.
questions.explanation: Policy is code-like; unsafe global rollout can amplify a mistake everywhere.

questions.question: What is the clearest sign that a control-plane design is bleeding into the data path incorrectly?
options: Request latency spikes whenever the central policy service is slow or unavailable.
Operators can explain decisions clearly.
Node caches hold versioned bundles.
Policies are compiled before distribution.
questions.explanation: The hot path should not depend synchronously on a centralized control service.

questions.question: Why might different policy classes need different degraded modes?
options: Availability and safety trade-offs differ between abuse controls, optimization hints, and customer-facing quotas.
All policies should always fail open.
All policies should always fail closed.
Policy classes do not affect risk.
questions.explanation: One blanket degraded-mode choice is often too coarse for mixed policy types.

questions.question: What is the strongest first move in this drill?
options: State explicitly what belongs in control plane versus data plane before drawing components.
Start by naming a vendor product.
Ignore versioning and talk only about throughput.
Assume the same failure mode for every policy.
questions.explanation: The interview value comes from making that boundary concrete and defensible.
